rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute severe CRPS-I flare in a limb injured 4 weeks earlier, with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen: afebrile, the affected hand is COLD not hot, and there is no spreading erythema, streaking or systemic sepsis. The source disposition is routine outpatient (GP review in 2 days). The correct telehealth action is prompt in-person GP review within 24–48 hours to confirm the diagnosis, clinically exclude joint/tendon-sheath infection, and start neuropathic analgesia — i.e. T2 (prompt GP review). The alarming autonomic features (tachycardia, presyncope-type dizziness, nausea) are pain-driven and, with a negative infective and vascular screen, do NOT by themselves mandate emergency escalation. Telehealth cannot palpate to fully exclude septic arthritis, so strong ED safety-netting (T4/T5) is mandatory — but that is safety-netting, not the baseline tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New fever, rigors, or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with a swollen painful hand shifts the picture toward septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis — a hand-surgical emergency requiring urgent assessment, aspiration and IV antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a fever, chills or shivering, or you start feeling generally unwell, go to an emergency department the same day — this needs to be checked urgently for infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The hand becomes hot and fiery red, redness spreads up the arm, red streaking appears, pus develops, or a hot swollen joint appears
escalation_edges.rationale: Advancing erythema, streaking, pus or a hot fluctuant joint indicates cellulitis/lymphangitis or septic arthritis rather than a sympathetic pain flare and requires urgent hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the hand turns hot and fiery red, the redness spreads up your arm, you see red streaks or pus, or a joint becomes hot and swollen — go straight to an emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The hand becomes pale, numb, or loses its pulse, or swelling becomes rapidly increasing and tensely tight
escalation_edges.rationale: A pale, pulseless or tensely swollen limb is acute limb ischaemia or compartment syndrome — a time-critical vascular emergency distinct from CRPS vasomotor colour change.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the hand goes pale or numb, you can't feel a pulse, or it swells up tight and hard very quickly — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Pain remains uncontrolled despite medication, or the patient cannot cope or manage safely alone
escalation_edges.rationale: Uncontrolled pain and inability to self-care in a person living alone warrants same-day urgent in-person review for analgesia and support, short of an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the medication isn't controlling the pain or you just can't manage at home on your own, contact your GP or an urgent care centre the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and cannot use the dominant hand — cannot open medication bottles, prepare food, or use the phone easily
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for arranging in-person review and practical support; ask whether anyone can check on her, arrange pre-opened medication organisers and urgent occupational-therapy input, and ensure she has a reliable way to call for help if she deteriorates.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: See a GP in person within the next 1–2 days to confirm the cause, examine the hand to rule out infection, and start nerve-pain medication. In the meantime keep the hand warm in a loose cotton glove and gently move the fingers through the day — do NOT use ice and do not strap the hand up tightly.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book an in-person GP appointment within 24–48 hours; for after-hours worsening call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage advice.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If infective red flags appear after hours (fever, hot spreading redness), go to your nearest emergency department; call 000 immediately if the hand becomes pale, numb or pulseless.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note the timeline (wrist injury about 4 weeks ago, sudden 12-hour flare), the colour and temperature changes, that ice made the pain worse, and any medications taken, to show the GP.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Assigning pure self-care with no medical review and no safety-net to an acute severe flare with an unexcludable infective mimic.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An early septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis evolves unwitnessed while the patient, alone and in an uncontrolled pain crisis, receives no review — risking joint destruction, sepsis, and progression to contracture.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Routine GP appointment in 1–2 weeks is too slow for an acute severe 12-hour flare that needs infection exclusion and prompt analgesia.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an afebrile, infection-screen-negative chronic-pain flare to ED or calling an ambulance as the baseline disposition — the telehealth can't-examine reflex driving unnecessary emergency escalation, resource waste and patient anxiety.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot palpate the hand to fully exclude septic arthritis / flexor tenosynovitis, or assess limb perfusion.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Arrange prompt (24–48 hour) in-person review to examine the hand and exclude infection, and safety-net explicitly for infective and vascular red flags with ED/000 escalation — rather than either dismissing the presentation or reflexively sending an afebrile, screen-negative patient to ED.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) confidently diagnosing a benign flare and giving pure self-care with no infection safety-net, or (b) defaulting to emergency ambulance/ED purely because the hand cannot be examined remotely, despite a negative infective and vascular screen.
